text:
The Gold Lender of Babylon 



Fifty pieces of gold! Never before had Rodan, the spearmaker of old Babylon, carried so much gold in his leather wallet. He strode happily down the king's highway, returning from the palace of his generous Majesty. The gold clinked cheerfully as the wallet at his belt swayed with each step. To him, it was the sweetest music he had ever heard.

Fifty pieces of gold! All his! He could hardly realize his good fortune. What power lay in those clinking discs! They could purchase anything he wanted. He could buy a grand house, land, cattle, camels, horses, or chariots. He could have whatever he desired.

What use should he make of it? This evening, as he turned into a side street toward his sister's home, he could think of nothing he would rather possess than those same glittering, heavy pieces of gold. They were his to keep.

A few evenings later, a confused Rodan entered the shop of Mathon, a lender of gold and a dealer in jewels and rare fabrics. Looking neither right nor left at the colorful, artfully displayed goods, he walked straight through to the living quarters at the back. There, he found the polite Mathon lounging on a rug. Mathon was enjoying a meal served by an enslaved Black man.
I need your advice, because I do not know what to do.

Rodan stood solidly with his feet apart. His hairy chest was exposed by the open front of his leather jacket.

Mathon's narrow, pale face smiled a friendly greeting.

What foolish things have you done that you should seek the lender of gold? Have you been unlucky at the gambling table? Or has some plump woman entangled you? I have known you for many years. Yet, you have never asked me to help you with your troubles.
No, no. It is nothing like that. I do not seek gold. Instead, I need your wise advice.
Hear! Hear! Listen to what this man says! No one comes to the lender of gold for advice. My ears must be deceiving me.
They hear the truth.

Can this be true? Rodan, the spearmaker, shows more cleverness than all the rest. He comes to Mathon not for gold, but for advice. Many men come to me for gold to pay for their foolish mistakes. But as for advice, they do not want it. Yet, who is more able to give advice than the lender of gold? After all, many men come to me when they are in trouble.
You shall eat with me, Rodan,
he continued.
You shall be my guest for the evening. Andol,
he commanded the enslaved servant,

roll out a rug for my friend, Rodan, the spearmaker, who comes for advice. He shall be my honored guest. Bring him plenty of food, and get him my largest cup. Choose the best wine carefully so that he may enjoy his drink.
Now, tell me what troubles you.
It is the king's gift.
The king's gift? The king gave you a gift, and it gives you trouble? What kind of gift is it?
The king was very pleased with the design I submitted for a new spearhead for the royal guard. Because of this, he presented me with fifty pieces of gold. Now, I am very confused.
Every hour of the day, I am begged by people who want to share it with me.
That is natural. More men want gold than have it. They expect someone who gets it easily to share it with them. But can you not just say 'No'? Is your will not as strong as your fist?
I can say no to many people. Yet, sometimes it would be easier to say yes. How can a man refuse to share with his sister, to whom he is deeply devoted?
Surely, your own sister would not want to stop you from enjoying your reward.

But it is for the sake of Araman, her husband. She wants to see him become a rich merchant. She feels that he has never had a fair chance. So, she begs me to loan him this gold. She hopes he can become a prosperous merchant and repay me from his profits.
My friend,
Mathon resumed,

you bring up a worthy subject to discuss. Gold brings its owner responsibility. It changes his position among his fellow men. It brings the fear that he might lose it, or that someone might trick him out of it. It brings a feeling of power and the ability to do good. At the same time, it brings opportunities where his very best intentions might get him into trouble.

Did you ever hear of the farmer from Nineveh who could understand the language of animals? I suppose not, because it is not the kind of story men usually tell over the bronze caster's forge. I will tell it to you. You should know that borrowing and lending involve much more than simply passing gold from one hand to another.

This farmer, who could understand what the animals said to each other, lingered in the farmyard each evening just to listen to their words. One evening, he heard the ox complaining to the donkey about his hard life. 'I labor pulling the plow from morning until night,' the ox said. 'No matter how hot the day is, how tired my legs are, or how the harness rubs my neck, I still must work. But you are a creature of leisure. You are draped with a colorful blanket, and you do nothing more than carry our master wherever he wants to go. When he goes nowhere, you rest and eat green grass all day long.'

Now, the donkey, in spite of his vicious kick, was a good-hearted fellow and sympathized with the ox. 'My good friend,' he replied, 'you do work very hard, and I would like to help ease your burden. Therefore, I will tell you how you can have a day of rest. In the morning, when the enslaved servant comes to fetch you for the plow, lie on the ground. Groan loudly so that he will think you are sick and cannot work.'
So, the ox took the donkey's advice. The next morning, the servant returned to the farmer and told him the ox was sick and could not pull the plow.
'Then,' said the farmer, 'hitch the donkey to the plow, because the plowing must go on.'

All that day, the donkey, who had only intended to help his friend, found himself forced to do the ox's job. When night came and he was released from the plow, his heart was bitter. His legs were tired, and his neck was sore where the harness had rubbed it.
The farmer lingered in the barnyard to listen.
The ox spoke first. 'You are a good friend. Because of your wise advice, I have enjoyed a day of rest.'

'And I,' replied the donkey, 'am like many other simplehearted fools who start out helping a friend and end up doing their work for them. From now on, you will pull your own plow. I heard the master tell the servant to send for the butcher if you were sick again. I hope he does, because you are a lazy fellow.' After that, they never spoke to each other again, and this ended their friendship. Can you tell me the moral of this story, Rodan?
It is a good story,
responded Rodan,
but I do not see the moral.

I did not think you would. But the moral is there, and it is simple, too. It is just this: If you want to help your friend, do so in a way that will not put your friend's burdens on your own shoulders.
I had not thought of that. It is a wise moral. I do not want to take on the burdens of my sister's husband. But tell me, you lend to many people. Do the borrowers not repay you?
Mathon smiled the smile of a man whose soul was rich with much experience.

Could a loan be considered good if the borrower cannot repay it? The lender must be wise and judge carefully. He must decide whether his gold can serve a useful purpose for the borrower and then return to him. Otherwise, it might be wasted by someone who cannot use it wisely. That would leave the lender without his treasure, and it would leave the borrower with a debt he cannot repay. I will show you the tokens in my token chest, and I will let them tell you some of their stories.

He brought a chest into the room. It was as long as his arm, covered in red pigskin, and decorated with bronze designs. He placed it on the floor and sat cross-legged in front of it, resting both hands on the lid.
Whenever I lend to someone, I require a token for my chest,
Mathon explained.
It stays here until the loan is paid off. When they repay me, I give the token back. But if they never repay, the token stays as a reminder of someone who broke my trust.

My token box shows that the safest loans are to people who own more than what they want to borrow. They own lands, jewels, camels, or other things that could be sold to repay the loan. Some of the tokens they give me are jewels worth more than the loan itself. Others are written promises to hand over property if they cannot repay on time. With these kinds of loans, I am confident my gold will be returned along with the interest, because the loan is backed by actual property.

The next safest loans go to those who have the ability to earn money. They are people like you, who work and get paid. They have a steady income. If they are honest and avoid bad luck, I know they can easily repay the gold I lend them, along with the interest I am owed. These loans are based on human effort.

Then there are those who have neither property nor a steady income. Life is hard, and some people simply cannot manage it. I regret making loans to them, even if it is just a single coin. My token box will likely remind me of that mistake for years to come, unless the loan is guaranteed by good friends who know the borrower is an honest person.

Mathon unfastened the clasp and opened the lid. Rodan leaned forward eagerly.

At the top of the chest, a bronze necklace rested on a red cloth. Mathon picked it up and patted it gently.

This will always stay in my token chest because its owner has passed away. I treasure his token and his memory, for he was a good friend. We did business together with great success. But one day, he brought back a bride from the East. She was beautiful, but a dazzling and different creature. He spent his gold carelessly to satisfy her every wish. When all his money was gone, he came to me in deep distress. I gave him advice and promised to help him regain control of his life. He swore by the sign of the Great Bull that he would. But it was not meant to be. During an argument, he dared her to pierce his heart, and she drove a knife right into it.
And what happened to her?
Rodan asked.
Ah, yes. This was hers.
Mathon picked up the red cloth.

Filled with bitter regret, she threw herself into the Euphrates River. Those two loans will never be repaid. The chest shows you, Rodan, that people caught up in intense emotions are never safe risks for a gold lender.
Now, this one is different,
Mathon said, reaching for a ring carved from ox bone.

This belongs to a farmer. I buy the rugs his women weave. Once, locusts destroyed his crops, and his family had no food. I lent him money, and he repaid me as soon as the new crop came in. Later, he returned with a story from a traveler about strange goats in a faraway land. These goats had long, soft hair that could be woven into the most beautiful rugs Babylon had ever seen. He wanted to buy a herd, but he had no money. So, I lent him the gold to make the trip and bring the goats back. His herd has now started to grow. Next year, I will surprise the wealthy lords of Babylon with the finest rugs they have ever bought. Soon, I will have to return his ring, because he always insists on paying his debts quickly.
Do some borrowers actually do that?
Rodan asked.

Yes, if they borrow money to start a profitable business, they usually do. But if they borrow to cover up their own foolish mistakes, I warn you to be very careful if you ever want to see your gold again.
Tell me about this one,
Rodan requested. He picked up a heavy gold bracelet, set with jewels in unique designs.
I see the ladies' jewelry catches your eye, my friend,
Mathon teased.
I am still much younger than you,
Rodan replied with a smile.
That may be true, but this time you are imagining romance where there is none,
Mathon said.

The owner of this bracelet is old, fat, and wrinkled. She talks so much, yet says so little, that she drives me completely crazy! Her family once had plenty of money and they were good customers, but hard times hit them. She has a son, and she desperately wants him to become a merchant. So, she came to me and borrowed gold to buy him a partnership with a caravan owner, someone who travels with camels and trades goods from city to city.

Unfortunately, that caravan owner turned out to be a scoundrel. He left the poor boy in a faraway city with no money and no friends, sneaking away early while the young man slept. Perhaps when this boy grows into a real man, he will repay the debt. Until then, I get no interest on the loan—just a lot of empty talk from his mother. But I must admit, the jewels in that bracelet are easily worth the gold I lent her.
Did this woman ask for your advice on whether the loan was a smart idea?
Rodan asked.
Quite the opposite,
Mathon sighed.

She had already pictured her son as a wealthy and powerful man of Babylon. Suggesting anything else only made her furious. She gave me a very harsh scolding! I knew it was a huge risk for such an inexperienced boy. However, since she offered excellent security with this bracelet, I could not refuse the loan.
Now, this,
Mathon continued, waving a piece of packing rope tied into a knot.

This belongs to Nebatur, the camel trader. Whenever he wants to buy a herd that costs more money than he currently has, he brings me this knot. I lend him whatever gold he needs. He is a very wise trader. I trust his good judgment, so I lend to him freely. Many other merchants in Babylon have earned my trust through their honorable behavior. Their tokens come and go frequently from my box. Good merchants are a huge asset to our city. It benefits me to help them keep their trade moving, which in turn keeps Babylon prosperous.
Mathon then picked out a beetle carved from turquoise. He tossed it onto the floor with disgust.
A bug from Egypt,
he muttered.

The young man who owns this does not care if I ever get my gold back. When I scold him, he simply replies, 'How can I repay you when bad luck follows me everywhere? Besides, you have plenty of money.' What can I do in this situation? The token actually belongs to his father. His father is a hardworking, honorable man with little money, but he pledged his own land and livestock to support his son's business dreams. The young man found some early success, but then he became far too eager to get rich quickly. He did not have enough knowledge or experience, and his businesses entirely collapsed. Youth is always ambitious. Young people want to take shortcuts to wealth and all the nice things that money can buy. In their rush to get rich quickly, they often borrow money very foolishly.

Because they lack life experience, young people do not realize that hopeless debt is like a deep, dark pit. You can fall into it very quickly, but you might struggle uselessly for years trying to climb out. It is a pit full of sorrow and regret. It blocks out the bright sunlight of your days and ruins your nights with restless tossing and turning. However, I do not discourage borrowing gold. In fact, I highly encourage it. I recommend borrowing, as long as it is for a smart and wise purpose. I actually achieved my own first real success as a merchant by using borrowed gold.

But what is a lender supposed to do in a situation like this? The young man is in total despair and gets nothing done. He is deeply discouraged, and he makes zero effort to repay the debt. At the same time, my heart simply cannot bear to take away his poor father's land and cattle.
You have told me many interesting things,
Rodan ventured,
but I still have not heard the answer to my question. Should I lend my fifty pieces of gold to my sister's husband? That money means a great deal to me.
Your sister is an excellent woman, and I have a lot of respect for her,
Mathon replied.
If her husband came to me asking to borrow fifty pieces of gold, the first thing I would ask is what he plans to do with the money.

If he told me he wanted to become a merchant like me, dealing in jewels and expensive furniture, I would ask him: 'What do you actually know about running a business? Do you know where to buy goods at the lowest cost? Do you know where to sell them at a fair and profitable price?' Could he honestly answer 'Yes' to those questions?
No, he could not,
Rodan admitted.
He has helped me a lot with making spears, and he has done some basic work in the shops.
Then I would tell him that his plan is not wise,
Mathon said.
Merchants must take the time to learn their trade. His ambition might be well-meaning, but it is just not practical. Because of that lack of experience, I would not lend him any gold.

But suppose he could say this: 'Yes, I have helped merchants for a long time. I know exactly how to travel to Smyrna and buy rugs at a low cost directly from the women who weave them. I also know many rich people here in Babylon, and I can sell those rugs to them for a large profit.' If he said that, I would tell him: 'Your plan is smart, and your ambition is honorable. I will gladly lend you the fifty pieces of gold, provided you can give me security to guarantee the money will be returned.' But what if he replied, 'I have no security to offer. All I have is my word as an honest man that I will pay you back well'? I would have to tell him, 'I treasure every single piece of my gold. If robbers attacked you on your way to Smyrna and stole the money, or if they took your rugs on the journey back, you would have absolutely no way to repay me. My gold would be lost forever.'

Gold, you see, Rodan, is my merchandise as a money lender. It is easy to lend. However, if it is lent unwisely, it is difficult to get back. A wise lender doesn't want the risk of the undertaking, but rather a guarantee of safe repayment.
It is good,
he continued,

to assist those in trouble. It is good to help those who have been struck down by fate. It is good to help those who are just starting out so they can make progress and become valuable citizens. But help must be given wisely. Otherwise, like the farmer's donkey, in our desire to help, we only end up taking on another person's burden.

I wandered from your question again, Rodan, but hear my answer: keep your fifty pieces of gold. What you earn from your labor and what you receive as a reward is your own. No one can force you to part with it unless it is your wish. If you want to lend it out to earn more gold, then lend it with caution and in many places. I do not like idle gold, but I like too much risk even less.
How many years have you worked as a spearmaker?
A full three years,
Rodan replied.
How much have you saved, besides the King's gift?
Three pieces of gold.
So each year you have worked, you have denied yourself good things just to save one piece of gold from your earnings?
That is true.
Then, through your self-denial, it would take you fifty years of labor to save fifty pieces of gold?
That would take a lifetime of labor.
Do you think your sister would want to risk fifty years' worth of your savings from the bronze melting pot just so her husband could experiment with being a merchant?
Not if I explained it in those words.

Then go to her and say: 'I have worked every day except fast days, from morning until night, for three years. I have denied myself many things my heart desired. For each year of hard labor and self-denial, I only have one piece of gold to show for it. You are my favorite sister, and I want your husband to engage in a business where he can prosper greatly. If he can submit a plan to me that seems wise and possible to my friend Mathon, then I will gladly lend him my entire savings from one year. That will give him the opportunity to prove he can succeed.' Say exactly that. If he has the soul to succeed within him, he will prove it. If he fails, he will not owe you more than he can hope to repay someday.

I am a gold lender because I own more gold than I can use in my own trade. I want my surplus gold to work for others and earn even more gold. I do not want to risk losing it, for I have worked hard and denied myself much to secure it. Therefore, I will no longer lend any of it where I am not confident that it is safe and will be returned to me. I will also not lend it where I am not convinced that the earnings will be paid to me promptly.

I have shared a few secrets from my token chest with you, Rodan. From them, you can understand the weakness of men. You can see their eagerness to borrow money that they have no certain way to repay. You can see how often their high hopes of making great earnings are simply false hopes. They just don't have the ability or training to fulfill them, even if they had the gold.

You now have gold, Rodan, which you should put to work earning more gold for yourself. You are about to become a gold lender, just like me. If you safely preserve your treasure, it will produce generous earnings for you. It will be a rich source of pleasure and profit for all your days. But if you let it slip away, it will be a source of constant sorrow and regret for as long as you live.
What do you desire most for this gold in your wallet?
To keep it safe.
Wisely spoken,
Mathon replied approvingly.
Your first desire is for safety. Do you think it would be truly safe from possible loss in the custody of your sister's husband?
I am afraid not, for he is not wise at guarding gold.

Then do not be swayed by foolish feelings of obligation. Do not trust your treasure to just anyone. If you want to help your family or friends, find ways other than risking the loss of your treasure. Do not forget that gold slips away in unexpected ways from those who are unskilled at guarding it. You might as well waste your treasure on extravagance as let someone else lose it for you.
What is your next desire for this treasure of yours, after safety?
That it earns more gold.

Again, you speak with wisdom. It should be put to work to earn and grow larger. Gold that is wisely lent out can even double itself through earnings before a young man like you grows old. But if you risk losing the gold itself, you also risk losing everything it could have earned.

Therefore, do not be swayed by the fantastic plans of impractical men. They think they see ways to force your gold to make unusually large earnings. Such plans are the creations of dreamers who are unskilled in the safe and dependable laws of trade. Be conservative in what you expect it to earn, so that you may keep and enjoy your treasure. To lend it out with a promise of sky-high returns is only inviting loss.

Seek to associate yourself with men and enterprises whose success is already established. That way, your treasure can earn generously under their skillful use, while being safely guarded by their wisdom and experience.
This way, you can avoid the misfortunes that follow most men who are entrusted with gold by the gods.
When Rodan tried to thank him for his wise advice, Mathon waved him off, saying,

The king's gift will teach you much wisdom. If you want to keep your fifty pieces of gold, you must be very discreet. Many potential uses will tempt you. Many people will offer you advice. Numerous opportunities to make large profits will be presented to you. The stories from my token box should serve as a warning. Before you let any piece of gold leave your pouch, you must be absolutely sure you have a safe way to pull it back again. If you ever want my further advice, please return. It is always gladly given.
Before you go, read what I have carved beneath the lid of my token box. It applies equally to both the borrower and the lender:
**BETTER A LITTLE CAUTION THAN A GREAT REGRET**
